title: Shoulder Dislocation
oneLiner: Anterior humeral head displacement from glenoid; common in young adults after fall or collision; must exclude neurovascular injury before and after reduction.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Axillary artery injury:** Expanding haematoma + absent radial pulse + pallor/paraesthesia — requires immediate vascular surgical referral; do not proceed with reduction until circulation is assessed.
- **Missed posterior dislocation:** Seen after seizure/electric shock; on single AP X-ray humeral head appears symmetrical (“light bulb sign”) but is locked posteriorly; needs axillary or trans-scapular view — if missed, can cause avascular necrosis of humeral head within weeks.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Sudden onset shoulder pain after acute injury (fall, collision, seizure, sports tackle)
  - Inability to move arm (especially abduction and external rotation)
  - Arm held in characteristic position: abducted and externally rotated (anterior) OR adducted and internally rotated (posterior)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Palpable “empty glenoid” anteriorly (anterior dislocation) or prominent humeral head posteriorly (posterior dislocation)
  - Loss of normal deltoid contour – “square shoulder” sign
  - Axillary nerve function: deltoid contraction + sensation over “soldier’s badge” area (upper lateral arm) – always document pre- and post-reduction
  - Radial pulse – compare with contralateral side
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Anterior dislocation (~95%)** – most common; arm held abducted/externally rotated; humeral head anterior on X-ray.
- **Posterior dislocation (~2-4%)** – arm adducted/internally rotated; humeral head posterior on scapular Y or axillary view; often missed on AP film.
- **Fracture of proximal humerus** – may present similarly but no “empty glenoid”; X-ray shows fracture line, not dislocation.
- **Rotator cuff tear** – acute pain + weakness; but shoulder not displaced; X-ray normal; ultrasound/MRI confirm.
- **Septic arthritis** – fever, erythema, severe pain with any movement, raised CRP/procalcitonin; joint aspiration needed.
- **Splitter:** “Arm position on presentation” – anterior dislocation causes arm abducted/externally rotated; posterior dislocation causes arm adducted/internally rotated.  
  If you can passively externally rotate the arm (without pain), it is **not** a dislocation.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Neurovascular examination: axillary nerve (sensation + motor), median/radial/ulnar nerves, radial pulse, capillary refill, skin colour and temperature
  - Document findings before any manipulation
- **Bloods:**  
  - Not routinely indicated unless suspect septic arthritis (CRP, blood cultures, joint aspiration) or significant trauma with other injuries (FBC, crossmatch)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** True AP + scapular Y (or axillary lateral) views – essential to confirm direction and exclude associated fracture
  - **Second-line:** CT shoulder if fracture-dislocation, complex humeral head fracture, or failed reduction on X-ray; CT also for posterior dislocation assessment
- **Special tests:**
  - Post-reduction X-ray (AP + Y view) to confirm concentric reduction and exclude iatrogenic fracture
  - If neurovascular deficit persists post-reduction: urgent arterial duplex or CT angiogram
- **Never-miss test:** **Pre- and post-reduction neurovascular exam** – omission is a serious error (axillary nerve palsy or vascular injury may be missed, leading to permanent deficit or limb loss).
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Adequate analgesia and sedation (morphine + midazolam IV, or propofol if anaesthetist present) – never attempt reduction without it
  - Closed reduction using **Stimson method** (prone, weight on wrist) or **Milch technique** (gentle abduction + external rotation) – avoid forceful traction (Kocher manoeuvre increases fracture risk)
  - After successful reduction: sling (collar and cuff) for comfort
  - **Do not immobilise in internal rotation** – use neutral rotation or external rotation splint if available (meta-analysis shows lower recurrence)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Usually not admitted; admit only if: (1) severe pain requiring IV analgesia, (2) associated fracture, (3) neurovascular compromise, (4) failed reduction requiring surgical intervention, (5) complex social situation
  - If admitted: sling immobilisation, regular neurovascular checks (hourly for first 6 hours, then 4-hourly), simple analgesia (paracetamol + ibuprofen)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - First-time dislocation in young (<25) – orthopaedic follow-up for consideration of early stabilisation surgery (risk of recurrence >70%)
  - Age >40 – early active mobilisation to prevent frozen shoulder; rotator cuff ultrasound if weakness persists at 2 weeks
  - Physiotherapy for rotator cuff strengthening and proprioception
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - VTE prophylaxis: not routinely needed unless immobile with other risk factors (follow local policy)
  - Diet/NBM: first hour NBM if sedation planned; after reduction, normal diet
  - Observations: neurovascular checks 4-hourly for 24 hours
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Analgesia for reduction:**  
  - Morphine IV 2.5–5 mg bolus, repeated up to 10 mg (titrate to pain) OR fentanyl 50–100 mcg IV  
  - PLUS midazolam IV 2–5 mg (or lorazepam sublingual 1–2 mg if IV access difficult) – **monitor respiratory rate, SaO₂, and have flumazenil available**  
- **Post-reduction analgesia:**  
  - Paracetamol 1 g PO/IV QDS  
  - Ibuprofen 400–600 mg PO TDS (max 2.4 g/day) – avoid if renal impairment or GI bleeding history  
- **Common alternative:**  
  - Codeine 30–60 mg PO QDS (only if paracetamol/ibuprofen insufficient – limited evidence)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - NSAIDs avoid if eGFR <30; paracetamol safe in renal impairment but caution in severe hepatic disease (reduce dose to 3 g/day)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Oversedation with midazolam or morphine** – can cause respiratory depression → have flumazenil (200 mcg IV) and naloxone (400 mcg IV) ready; never give midazolam without monitoring and reversal agent at bedside
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Axillary nerve palsy (commonest nerve injury – usually neuropraxia, recovers in 6–12 weeks)  
  - Axillary artery injury (rare but limb-threatening – requires urgent vascular repair)  
  - Iatrogenic fracture of humeral head/glenoid (from forceful reduction)  
  - Failed reduction (consider interposed soft tissue – call senior)  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Recurrent dislocation (especially in young patients; Bankart lesion, Hill-Sachs lesion)  
  - Rotator cuff tear (especially in >40 years – may require repair)  
  - Frozen shoulder (adhesive capsulitis) – more common if immobilised >3 weeks in elderly  
  - Osteoarthritis of glenohumeral joint (after multiple dislocations or severe primary injury)
sections.Prognosis:
- First anterior dislocation: recurrence rate >70% in patients <20 years; only 10–15% in those >40 years.  
- Posterior dislocation: higher risk of avascular necrosis if diagnosis delayed >4 weeks (20–30%).  
- With prompt reduction and appropriate rehab, most patients regain functional range of motion within 6 weeks.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always document neurovascular status before AND after reduction** – use a diagram or chart.  
- **Repeat X-ray after every reduction attempt** – to confirm concentric reduction and exclude iatrogenic fracture.  
- **In elderly patients, start gentle passive range-of-motion exercises at 1 week** (with physiotherapy) to prevent frozen shoulder.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not use excessive force during reduction** – the Kocher manoeuvre (levering humerus with forearm) can fracture the humeral neck; use Stimson or Milch instead.  
- **Do not assume all dislocations are anterior** – always check the Y view to exclude posterior dislocation (especially after seizure).  
- **Do not immobilise for >3 weeks in elderly** – early mobilisation reduces risk of frozen shoulder; sling for comfort only after first week.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - Severe pain not controlled with simple analgesia  
  - Numbness, tingling, or weakness in arm/hand  
  - Inability to move shoulder or signs of infection (redness, fever)  
  - Re-dislocation during the first week  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Orthopaedic clinic in 2–4 weeks (or sooner if recurrent dislocation)  
  - Physiotherapy referral for rehabilitation
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior orthopaedic or ED consultant if:**  
  - Cannot reduce after **2 attempts**  
  - Persistent neurovascular deficit (absent pulse, severe numbness, motor loss) post-reduction  
  - Fracture-dislocation (e.g., greater tuberosity fracture, humeral neck fracture)  
  - Posterior dislocation uncertain on plain X-ray (needs CT)  
- **ICU/crash team criteria:**  
  - Sedation-related respiratory depression (RR <8, SpO₂ <90% despite O₂) – need bag-mask ventilation and reversal agents  
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or lactate >4 mmol/L with suspected vascular injury – escalate immediately

rawMarkdown:
## Shoulder Dislocation
**One-liner:** Anterior humeral head displacement from glenoid; common in young adults after fall or collision; must exclude neurovascular injury before and after reduction.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Axillary artery injury:** Expanding haematoma + absent radial pulse + pallor/paraesthesia — requires immediate vascular surgical referral; do not proceed with reduction until circulation is assessed.
- **Missed posterior dislocation:** Seen after seizure/electric shock; on single AP X-ray humeral head appears symmetrical (“light bulb sign”) but is locked posteriorly; needs axillary or trans-scapular view — if missed, can cause avascular necrosis of humeral head within weeks.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Sudden onset shoulder pain after acute injury (fall, collision, seizure, sports tackle)
  - Inability to move arm (especially abduction and external rotation)
  - Arm held in characteristic position: abducted and externally rotated (anterior) OR adducted and internally rotated (posterior)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Palpable “empty glenoid” anteriorly (anterior dislocation) or prominent humeral head posteriorly (posterior dislocation)
  - Loss of normal deltoid contour – “square shoulder” sign
  - Axillary nerve function: deltoid contraction + sensation over “soldier’s badge” area (upper lateral arm) – always document pre- and post-reduction
  - Radial pulse – compare with contralateral side

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Anterior dislocation (~95%)** – most common; arm held abducted/externally rotated; humeral head anterior on X-ray.
- **Posterior dislocation (~2-4%)** – arm adducted/internally rotated; humeral head posterior on scapular Y or axillary view; often missed on AP film.
- **Fracture of proximal humerus** – may present similarly but no “empty glenoid”; X-ray shows fracture line, not dislocation.
- **Rotator cuff tear** – acute pain + weakness; but shoulder not displaced; X-ray normal; ultrasound/MRI confirm.
- **Septic arthritis** – fever, erythema, severe pain with any movement, raised CRP/procalcitonin; joint aspiration needed.
- **Splitter:** “Arm position on presentation” – anterior dislocation causes arm abducted/externally rotated; posterior dislocation causes arm adducted/internally rotated.  
  If you can passively externally rotate the arm (without pain), it is **not** a dislocation.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Neurovascular examination: axillary nerve (sensation + motor), median/radial/ulnar nerves, radial pulse, capillary refill, skin colour and temperature
  - Document findings before any manipulation
- **Bloods:**  
  - Not routinely indicated unless suspect septic arthritis (CRP, blood cultures, joint aspiration) or significant trauma with other injuries (FBC, crossmatch)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** True AP + scapular Y (or axillary lateral) views – essential to confirm direction and exclude associated fracture
  - **Second-line:** CT shoulder if fracture-dislocation, complex humeral head fracture, or failed reduction on X-ray; CT also for posterior dislocation assessment
- **Special tests:**
  - Post-reduction X-ray (AP + Y view) to confirm concentric reduction and exclude iatrogenic fracture
  - If neurovascular deficit persists post-reduction: urgent arterial duplex or CT angiogram
- **Never-miss test:** **Pre- and post-reduction neurovascular exam** – omission is a serious error (axillary nerve palsy or vascular injury may be missed, leading to permanent deficit or limb loss).

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Adequate analgesia and sedation (morphine + midazolam IV, or propofol if anaesthetist present) – never attempt reduction without it
  - Closed reduction using **Stimson method** (prone, weight on wrist) or **Milch technique** (gentle abduction + external rotation) – avoid forceful traction (Kocher manoeuvre increases fracture risk)
  - After successful reduction: sling (collar and cuff) for comfort
  - **Do not immobilise in internal rotation** – use neutral rotation or external rotation splint if available (meta-analysis shows lower recurrence)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Usually not admitted; admit only if: (1) severe pain requiring IV analgesia, (2) associated fracture, (3) neurovascular compromise, (4) failed reduction requiring surgical intervention, (5) complex social situation
  - If admitted: sling immobilisation, regular neurovascular checks (hourly for first 6 hours, then 4-hourly), simple analgesia (paracetamol + ibuprofen)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - First-time dislocation in young (<25) – orthopaedic follow-up for consideration of early stabilisation surgery (risk of recurrence >70%)
  - Age >40 – early active mobilisation to prevent frozen shoulder; rotator cuff ultrasound if weakness persists at 2 weeks
  - Physiotherapy for rotator cuff strengthening and proprioception
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - VTE prophylaxis: not routinely needed unless immobile with other risk factors (follow local policy)
  - Diet/NBM: first hour NBM if sedation planned; after reduction, normal diet
  - Observations: neurovascular checks 4-hourly for 24 hours

### Drugs & Doses
- **Analgesia for reduction:**  
  - Morphine IV 2.5–5 mg bolus, repeated up to 10 mg (titrate to pain) OR fentanyl 50–100 mcg IV  
  - PLUS midazolam IV 2–5 mg (or lorazepam sublingual 1–2 mg if IV access difficult) – **monitor respiratory rate, SaO₂, and have flumazenil available**  
- **Post-reduction analgesia:**  
  - Paracetamol 1 g PO/IV QDS  
  - Ibuprofen 400–600 mg PO TDS (max 2.4 g/day) – avoid if renal impairment or GI bleeding history  
- **Common alternative:**  
  - Codeine 30–60 mg PO QDS (only if paracetamol/ibuprofen insufficient – limited evidence)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - NSAIDs avoid if eGFR <30; paracetamol safe in renal impairment but caution in severe hepatic disease (reduce dose to 3 g/day)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Oversedation with midazolam or morphine** – can cause respiratory depression → have flumazenil (200 mcg IV) and naloxone (400 mcg IV) ready; never give midazolam without monitoring and reversal agent at bedside

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Axillary nerve palsy (commonest nerve injury – usually neuropraxia, recovers in 6–12 weeks)  
  - Axillary artery injury (rare but limb-threatening – requires urgent vascular repair)  
  - Iatrogenic fracture of humeral head/glenoid (from forceful reduction)  
  - Failed reduction (consider interposed soft tissue – call senior)  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Recurrent dislocation (especially in young patients; Bankart lesion, Hill-Sachs lesion)  
  - Rotator cuff tear (especially in >40 years – may require repair)  
  - Frozen shoulder (adhesive capsulitis) – more common if immobilised >3 weeks in elderly  
  - Osteoarthritis of glenohumeral joint (after multiple dislocations or severe primary injury)

### Prognosis
- First anterior dislocation: recurrence rate >70% in patients <20 years; only 10–15% in those >40 years.  
- Posterior dislocation: higher risk of avascular necrosis if diagnosis delayed >4 weeks (20–30%).  
- With prompt reduction and appropriate rehab, most patients regain functional range of motion within 6 weeks.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always document neurovascular status before AND after reduction** – use a diagram or chart.  
- **Repeat X-ray after every reduction attempt** – to confirm concentric reduction and exclude iatrogenic fracture.  
- **In elderly patients, start gentle passive range-of-motion exercises at 1 week** (with physiotherapy) to prevent frozen shoulder.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not use excessive force during reduction** – the Kocher manoeuvre (levering humerus with forearm) can fracture the humeral neck; use Stimson or Milch instead.  
- **Do not assume all dislocations are anterior** – always check the Y view to exclude posterior dislocation (especially after seizure).  
- **Do not immobilise for >3 weeks in elderly** – early mobilisation reduces risk of frozen shoulder; sling for comfort only after first week.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  - Severe pain not controlled with simple analgesia  
  - Numbness, tingling, or weakness in arm/hand  
  - Inability to move shoulder or signs of infection (redness, fever)  
  - Re-dislocation during the first week  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Orthopaedic clinic in 2–4 weeks (or sooner if recurrent dislocation)  
  - Physiotherapy referral for rehabilitation

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior orthopaedic or ED consultant if:**  
  - Cannot reduce after **2 attempts**  
  - Persistent neurovascular deficit (absent pulse, severe numbness, motor loss) post-reduction  
  - Fracture-dislocation (e.g., greater tuberosity fracture, humeral neck fracture)  
  - Posterior dislocation uncertain on plain X-ray (needs CT)  
- **ICU/crash team criteria:**  
  - Sedation-related respiratory depression (RR <8, SpO₂ <90% despite O₂) – need bag-mask ventilation and reversal agents  
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or lactate >4 mmol/L with suspected vascular injury – escalate immediately
